name: Restore The Compact 128x128 PTX Grouped-Rows-4 Anchor After The Failed 256x128 Reopen

summary.name: Restore The Compact 128x128 PTX Grouped-Rows-4 Anchor After The Failed 256x128 Reopen
summary.hypothesis: The latest 256x128 measurement is a full structural regression, not a local timing miss. Restoring the compact 128x128 PTX microkernel dispatch and grouped_rows=4 anchor is the shortest path back to the last known good surface before any further frontier experiment is layered on top.
summary.expected_bottleneck: The immediate problem is the bad 256x128 hot-band geometry itself, which inflated shared-memory footprint and collapsed tensor throughput.
summary.risk: Low. This is a bounded dispatch restore back to the last measured compact anchor and does not require new synchronization logic.
ranking_notes: The latest run regressed by 5.49 ms, so recovering the last known good compact PTX anchor is more valuable than forcing another speculative change on top of a proven-bad geometry.
This is the cleanest single-variable recovery because the code change is only the hot-band dispatch choice.
Once restored, the compact surface can immediately resume frontier search from a measured anchor instead of a contaminated loss state.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if restoring the dispatch requires reviving any non-writer-safe code path beyond the exact compact PTX launch already measured at 24.689153 ms.
expected_local_changes: Switch the fixed hot-band dispatch back from the writer-safe 256x128 kernel to the compact 128x128 PTX microkernel.
Keep the peeled 64x384 row band and 64x96 tail unchanged.
Leave grouped_rows=4 and the accepted compact cadence intact on the recovery measurement.

notes: Build passed. Node A must measure this implementation next.
